title: Efficiently Modeling Long Sequences with Structured State Spaces

paragraphs.text:
Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## EFFICIENTLY MODELING LONG SEQUENCES WITH STRUCTURED STATE SPACES

## **Albert Gu & Karan Goel & Christopher R´e**

Department of Computer Science, Stanford University

_{_ albertgu,krng _}_ @stanford.edu, chrismre@cs.stanford.edu

## ABSTRACT

A central goal of sequence modeling is designing a single principled model that can address sequence data across a range of modalities and tasks, particularly on long-range dependencies. Although conventional models including RNNs, CNNs, and Transformers have specialized variants for capturing long dependencies, they still struggle to scale to very long sequences of 10000 or more steps. A promising recent approach proposed modeling sequences by simulating the fundamental state space model (SSM) _x[′]_ ( _t_ ) = _Ax_ ( _t_ ) + _Bu_ ( _t_ ) _, y_ ( _t_ ) = _Cx_ ( _t_ ) + _Du_ ( _t_ ), and showed that for appropriate choices of the state matrix _A_ , this system could handle longrange dependencies mathematically and empirically. However, this method has prohibitive computation and memory requirements, rendering it infeasible as a general sequence modeling solution. We propose the Structured State Space (S4) sequence model based on a new parameterization for the SSM, and show that it can be computed much more efficiently than prior approaches while preserving their theoretical strengths. Our technique involves conditioning _A_ with a low-rank correction, allowing it to be diagonalized stably and reducing the SSM to the well-studied computation of a Cauchy kernel. S4 achieves strong empirical results across a diverse range of established benchmarks, including (i) 91% accuracy on sequential CIFAR-10 with no data augmentation or auxiliary losses, on par with a larger 2-D ResNet, (ii) substantially closing the gap to Transformers on image and language modeling tasks, while performing generation 60 _×_ faster (iii) SoTA on every task from the Long Range Arena benchmark, including solving the challenging Path-X task of length 16k that all prior work fails on, while being as efficient as all competitors.[1]

## 1 INTRODUCTION

A central problem in sequence modeling is efficiently handling data that contains long-range dependencies (LRDs). Real-world time-series data often requires reasoning over tens of thousands of time steps, while few sequence models address even thousands of time steps. For instance, results from the long-range arena (LRA) benchmark (Tay et al., 2021) highlight that sequence models today perform poorly on LRD tasks, including one (Path-X) where no model performs better than random guessing.

Since LRDs are perhaps the foremost challenge for sequence models, all standard model families such as continuous-time models (CTMs), RNNs, CNNs, and Transformers include many specialized variants designed to address them. Modern examples include orthogonal and Lipschitz RNNs (Arjovsky et al., 2016; Erichson et al., 2021) to combat vanishing gradients, dilated convolutions to increase context size (Bai et al., 2018; Oord et al., 2016), and an increasingly vast family of efficient Transformers that reduce the quadratic dependence on sequence length (Katharopoulos et al., 2020; Choromanski et al., 2020). Despite being designed for LRDs, these solutions still perform poorly on challenging benchmarks such as LRA (Tay et al., 2021) or raw audio classification (Gu et al., 2021).

An alternative approach to LRDs was recently introduced based on the **state space model (SSM)** (Fig. 1). SSMs are a foundational scientific model used in fields such as control theory, computational neuroscience, and many more, but have not been applicable to deep learning for concrete theoretical reasons. In particular, Gu et al. (2021) showed that deep SSMs actually struggle even on simple tasks, but can perform exceptionally well when equipped with special state matrices _**A**_ recently derived

Figure 1: ( **Left** ) State Space Models (SSM) parameterized by matrices _**A** ,_ _**B** ,_ _**C** ,_ _**D**_ map an input signal _u_ ( _t_ ) to output _y_ ( _t_ ) through a latent state _x_ ( _t_ ). ( **Center** ) Recent theory on continuous-time memorization derives special _**A**_ matrices that allow SSMs to capture LRDs mathematically and empirically. ( **Right** ) SSMs can be computed either as a recurrence (left) or convolution (right). However, materializing these conceptual views requires utilizing different representations of its parameters (red, blue, green) which are very expensive to compute. S4 introduces a novel parameterization that efficiently swaps between these representations, allowing it to handle a wide range of tasks, be efficient at both training and inference, and excel at long sequences.

to solve a problem of continuous-time memorization (Voelker et al., 2019; Gu et al., 2020a). Their Linear State Space Layer (LSSL) conceptually unifies the strengths of CTM, RNN and CNN models, and provides a proof of concept that deep SSMs can address LRDs in principle.

Unfortunately, the LSSL is infeasible to use in practice because of prohibitive computation and memory requirements induced by the state representation. For state dimension _N_ and sequence length _L_ , computing the latent state requires _O_ ( _N_[2] _L_ ) operations and _O_ ( _NL_ ) space – compared to a Ω( _L_ + _N_ ) lower bound for both. Thus for reasonably sized models (e.g. _N_ = 256 in Gu et al. (2021)), the LSSL uses orders of magnitude more memory than comparably-sized RNNs or CNNs. Although theoretically efficient algorithms for the LSSL were proposed, we show that these are numerically unstable. In particular, the special _**A**_ matrix is highly non-normal in the linear algebraic sense, which prevents the application of conventional algorithmic techniques. Consequently, although the LSSL showed that SSMs have strong performance, they are currently computationally impractical as a general sequence modeling solution.

In this work, we introduce the **Structured State Space (S4)** sequence model based on the SSM that solves the critical computational bottleneck in previous work. Technically, S4 reparameterizes the structured state matrices _**A**_ appearing in Voelker et al. (2019); Gu et al. (2020a) by decomposing them as the sum of a low-rank and skew-symmetric term. Additionally, instead of expanding the standard SSM in coefficient space, we compute its truncated generating function in frequency space, which can be simplified into a multipole-like evaluation. Combining these two ideas, we show that the low-rank term can be corrected by the Woodbury identity while the skew-symmetric term can be diagonalized stably, ultimately reducing to a well-studied and theoretically stable Cauchy kernel (Pan, 2001; 2017). This results in _O_[˜] ( _N_ + _L_ ) computation and _O_ ( _N_ + _L_ ) memory usage, which is essentially tight for sequence models. Compared to the LSSL, S4 is up to 30 _×_ faster with 400 _×_ less memory usage, while exceeding the LSSL’s performance empirically.

Empirically, S4 significantly advances the state-of-the-art for LRD. On the LRA benchmark for efficient sequence models, S4 is as fast as all baselines while outperforming them by 20+ points on average. S4 is the first model to solve the difficult LRA Path-X task (length-16384), achieving **88% accuracy compared to 50% random guessing** for all prior work. On speech classification with length-16000 sequences, S4 halves the test error (1 _._ 7%) of specialized Speech CNNs – by contrast, all RNN and Transformer baselines fail to learn ( _≥_ 70% error).

**Towards a general-purpose sequence model.** Beyond LRD, a broad goal of machine learning is to develop a single model that can be used across a wide range of problems. Models today are typically specialized to solve problems from a particular domain (e.g. images, audio, text, time-series), and enable a narrow range of capabilities (e.g. efficient training, fast generation, handling irregularly sampled data). This specialization is typically expressed via domain-specific preprocessing, inductive biases, and architectures. Sequence models provide a general framework for solving many of these problems with reduced specialization – e.g. Vision Transformers for image classification with less

2

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

2D information (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020). However, most models such as Transformers generally still require substantial specialization per task to achieve high performance.

Deep SSMs in particular have conceptual strengths that suggest they may be promising as a general sequence modeling solution. These strengths include a principled approach to handling LRDs, as well as the ability to move between continuous-time, convolutional, and recurrent model representations, each with distinct capabilities (Fig. 1). Our technical contributions enable SSMs to be applied successfully to a varied set of benchmarks with minimal modification:

- _Large-scale generative modeling._ On CIFAR-10 density estimation, S4 is competitive with the best autoregressive models (2 _._ 85 bits per dim). On WikiText-103 language modeling, S4 substantially closes the gap to Transformers (within 0 _._ 8 perplexity), setting SoTA for attention-free models.
- _Fast autoregressive generation._ Like RNNs, S4 can use its latent state to perform 60 _×_ faster pixel/token generation than standard autoregressive models on CIFAR-10 and WikiText-103.
- _Sampling resolution change._ Like specialized CTMs, S4 can adapt to changes in time-series sampling frequency without retraining, e.g. at 0 _._ 5 _×_ frequency on speech classification.

- _Learning with weaker inductive biases._ With no architectural changes, S4 surpasses Speech CNNs on speech classification, outperforms the specialized Informer model on time-series forecasting problems, and matches a 2-D ResNet on sequential CIFAR with over 90% accuracy.

## 2 BACKGROUND: STATE SPACES

Sections 2.1 to 2.4 describe the four properties of SSMs in Fig. 1: the classic continuous-time representation, addressing LRDs with the HiPPO framework, the discrete-time recurrent representation, and the parallelizable convolution representation. In particular, Section 2.4 introduces the SSM convolution kernel _**K**_ , which is the focus of our theoretical contributions in Section 3.

## 2.1 STATE SPACE MODELS: A CONTINUOUS-TIME LATENT STATE MODEL

The state space model is defined by the simple equation (1). It maps a 1-D input signal _u_ ( _t_ ) to an _N_ -D latent state _x_ ( _t_ ) before projecting to a 1-D output signal _y_ ( _t_ ).

SSMs are broadly used in many scientific disciplines and related to latent state models such as Hidden Markov Models (HMM). Our goal is to simply use the SSM as a black-box representation in a deep sequence model, where _**A** ,_ _**B** ,_ _**C** ,_ _**D**_ are parameters learned by gradient descent. For the remainder of this paper, we will omit the parameter _**D**_ for exposition (or equivalently, assume _**D**_ = 0) because the term _**D** u_ can be viewed as a skip connection and is easy to compute.

## 2.2 ADDRESSING LONG-RANGE DEPENDENCIES WITH HIPPO

Prior work found that the basic SSM (1) actually performs very poorly in practice. Intuitively, one explanation is that linear first-order ODEs solve to an exponential function, and thus may suffer from gradients scaling exponentially in the sequence length (i.e., the vanishing/exploding gradients problem (Pascanu et al., 2013)). To address this problem, the LSSL leveraged the HiPPO theory of continuous-time memorization (Gu et al., 2020a). HiPPO specifies a class of certain matrices _**A** ∈_ R _[N][×][N]_ that when incorporated into (1), allows the state _x_ ( _t_ ) to memorize the history of the input _u_ ( _t_ ). The most important matrix in this class is defined by equation (2), which we will call the HiPPO matrix. For example, the LSSL found that simply modifying an SSM from a random matrix _**A**_ to equation (2) improved its performance on the sequential MNIST benchmark from 60% to 98%.

## 2.3 DISCRETE-TIME SSM: THE RECURRENT REPRESENTATION

To be applied on a discrete input sequence ( _u_ 0 _, u_ 1 _, . . ._ ) instead of continuous function _u_ ( _t_ ), (1) must be discretized by a **step size** ∆ that represents the resolution of the input. Conceptually, the inputs _uk_ can be viewed as sampling an implicit underlying continuous signal _u_ ( _t_ ), where _uk_ = _u_ ( _k_ ∆).
To discretize the continuous-time SSM, we follow prior work in using the bilinear method (Tustin, 1947), which converts the state matrix _**A**_ into an approximation _**A**_ . The discrete SSM is

Equation (3) is now a _sequence-to-sequence_ map _uk �→ yk_ instead of function-to-function. Moreover the state equation is now a recurrence in _xk_ , allowing the discrete SSM to be computed like an RNN. Concretely, _xk ∈_ R _[N]_ can be viewed as a _hidden state_ with transition matrix _**A**_ .

Notationally, throughout this paper we use _**A** ,_ _**B** , . . ._ to denote discretized SSM matrices defined by (3). Note that these matrices are a function of both _**A**_ as well as a step size ∆; we suppress this dependence for notational convenience when it is clear.

## 2.4 TRAINING SSMS: THE CONVOLUTIONAL REPRESENTATION

The recurrent SSM (3) is not practical for training on modern hardware due to its sequentiality. Instead, there is a well-known connection between linear time-invariant (LTI) SSMs such as (1) and continuous convolutions. Correspondingly, (3) can actually be written as a discrete convolution.

For simplicity let the initial state be _x−_ 1 = 0. Then unrolling (3) explicitly yields

This can be vectorized into a convolution (4) with an explicit formula for the convolution kernel (5).

In other words, equation (4) is a single (non-circular) convolution and can be computed very efficiently with FFTs, _provided_ that _**K**_ is known. However, computing _**K**_ in (5) is non-trivial and is the focus of our technical contributions in Section 3. We call _**K**_ the **SSM convolution kernel**

3 METHOD: STRUCTURED STATE SPACES (S4)

Our technical results focus on developing the S4 parameterization and showing how to efficiently compute all views of the SSM (Section 2): the continuous representation ( _**A** ,_ _**B** ,_ _**C**_ ) (1), the recurrent representation ( _**A** ,_ _**B** ,_ _**C**_ ) (3), and the convolutional representation _**K**_ (4).

Section 3.1 motivates our approach, which is based on the linear algebraic concepts of conjugation and diagonalization, and discusses why the naive application of this approach does not work. Section 3.2 gives an overview of the key technical components of our approach and formally defines the S4 parameterization. Section 3.3 sketches the main results, showing that S4 is asymptotically efficient (up to log factors) for sequence models. Proofs are in Appendices B and C.

## 3.1 MOTIVATION: DIAGONALIZATION

The fundamental bottleneck in computing the discrete-time SSM (3) is that it involves repeated matrix multiplication by _**A**_ . For example, computing (5) naively as in the LSSL involves _L_ successive multiplications by _**A**_ , requiring _O_ ( _N_[2] _L_ ) operations and _O_ ( _NL_ ) space.

To overcome this bottleneck, we use a structural result that allows us to simplify SSMs.

After multiplying the right side SSM by _**V**_ , the two SSMs become identical with _x_ = _**V** x_ ˜. Therefore these compute the exact same operator _u �→ y_ , but with a change of basis by _**V**_ in the state _x_ .

Lemma 3.1 motivates putting _**A**_ into a canonical form by conjugation[2] , which is ideally more structured and allows faster computation. For example, if _**A**_ were diagonal, the resulting computations become much more tractable. In particular, the desired _**K**_ (equation (4)) would be a **Vandermonde product** which theoretically only needs _O_ (( _N_ + _L_ ) log[2] ( _N_ + _L_ )) arithmetic operations (Pan, 2001).

Unfortunately, the naive application of diagonalization does not work due to numerical issues. First, Vandermonde multiplication is itself a famously ill-conditioned problem (Pan, 2016). Furthermore, we derive the explicit diagonalization for the HiPPO matrix (2) and show it has entries exponentially large in the state size _N_ , rendering the diagonalization numerically infeasible (e.g. _**CV**_ in Lemma 3.1 would not be computable). We note that Gu et al. (2021) proposed a different (unimplemented) algorithm to compute _**K**_ faster than the naive algorithm. In Appendix B, we prove that it is also numerically unstable for related reasons.

- 3.2 THE S4 PARAMETERIZATION: NORMAL PLUS LOW-RANK

The previous discussion implies that we should only conjugate by well-conditioned matrices _**V**_ . The ideal scenario is when the matrix _**A**_ is diagonalizable by a perfectly conditioned (i.e., unitary) matrix. By the Spectral Theorem of linear algebra, this is exactly the class of **normal matrices** . However, this class of matrices is restrictive; in particular, it does not contain the HiPPO matrix (2).

We make the observation that although the HiPPO matrix is not normal, it can be decomposed as the _sum of a normal and low-rank matrix_ . However, this is still not useful by itself: unlike a diagonal matrix, powering up this sum (in (5)) is still slow and not easily optimized. We overcome this bottleneck by simultaneously applying three new techniques.

- Instead of computing _**K**_ directly, we compute its spectrum by evaluating its **truncated generating function**[�] _[L] j_ =0 _[−]_[1] _**K** jζ[j]_ at the roots of unity _ζ_ . _**K**_ can then be found by applying an inverse FFT.

- This generating function is closely related to the matrix resolvent, and now involves a matrix _inverse_ instead of _power_ . The low-rank term can now be corrected by applying the **Woodbury identity** which reduces ( _**A**_ + _**P Q**[∗]_ ) _[−]_[1] in terms of _**A**[−]_[1] , truly reducing to the diagonal case.
- Finally, we show that the diagonal matrix case is equivalent to the computation of a **Cauchy kernel** 1

## 3.3 S4 ALGORITHMS AND COMPUTATIONAL COMPLEXITY

By equation (6), note that NPLR matrices can be conjugated into _diagonal plus low-rank_ (DPLR) form (now over C instead of R ). Theorems 2 and 3 describe the complexities of SSMs where _**A**_ is in DPLR form. S4 is optimal or near-optimal for both recurrent and convolutional representations. **Theorem 2** (S4 Recurrence) **.** _Given any step size_ ∆ _, computing one step of the recurrence_ (3) _can be done in O_ ( _N_ ) _operations where N is the state size._

Theorem 2 follows from the fact that the inverse of a DPLR matrix is also DPLR (e.g. also by the Woodbury identity). This implies that the discretized matrix _**A**_ is the product of two DPLR matrices and thus has _O_ ( _N_ ) matrix-vector multiplication. Appendix C.2 computes _**A**_ in closed DPLR form. **Theorem 3** (S4 Convolution) **.** _Given any step size_ ∆ _, computing the SSM convolution filter_ _**K** can be reduced to 4 Cauchy multiplies, requiring only O_[�] ( _N_ + _L_ ) _operations and O_ ( _N_ + _L_ ) _space._

Appendix C, Definition 3 formally defines Cauchy matrices, which are related to rational interpolation problems. Computing with Cauchy matrices is an extremely well-studied problem in numerical analysis, with both fast arithmetic and numerical algorithms based on the famous Fast Multipole Method (FMM) (Pan, 2001; 2015; 2017). The computational complexities of these algorithms under various settings are described in Appendix C, Proposition 5.
We reiterate that Theorem 3 is our core technical contribution, and its algorithm is the very motivation of the NPLR S4 parameterization. This algorithm is formally sketched in Algorithm 1.

## 3.4 ARCHITECTURE DETAILS OF THE DEEP S4 LAYER

Concretely, an S4 layer is parameterized as follows. First initialize a SSM with _**A**_ set to the HiPPO matrix (2). By Lemma 3.1 and Theorem 1, this SSM is unitarily equivalent to some ( **Λ** _−_ _**P Q**[∗] ,_ _**B** ,_ _**C**_ ) for some diagonal **Λ** and vectors _**P** ,_ _**Q** ,_ _**B** ,_ _**C** ∈_ C _[N][×]_[1] . These comprise S4’s 5 _N_ trainable parameters.

The overall deep neural network (DNN) architecture of S4 is similar to prior work. As defined above, S4 defines a map from R _[L] →_ R _[L]_ , i.e. a 1-D sequence map. Typically, DNNs operate on feature maps of size _H_ instead of 1. S4 handles multiple features by simply defining _H_ independent copies of itself, and then mixing the _H_ features with a position-wise linear layer for a total of _O_ ( _H_[2] )+ _O_ ( _HN_ ) parameters per layer. Nonlinear activation functions are also inserted between these layers. Overall, S4 defines a sequence-to-sequence map of shape (batch size, sequence length, hidden dimension), exactly the same as related sequence models such as Transformers, RNNs, and CNNs.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

Section 4.1 benchmarks S4 against the LSSL and efficient Transformer models. Section 4.2 validates S4 on LRDs: the LRA benchmark and raw speech classification. Section 4.3 investigates whether S4 can be used as a general sequence model to perform effectively and efficiently in a wide variety of settings including image classification, image and text generation, and time series forecasting.

## 4.1 S4 EFFICIENCY BENCHMARKS

We benchmark that S4 can be trained quickly and efficiently, both compared to the LSSL, as well as efficient Transformer variants designed for long-range sequence modeling. As outlined in Section 3, S4 is theoretically much more efficient than the LSSL, and Table 1 confirms that the S4 is orders of magnitude more speed- and memory-efficient for practical layer sizes. In fact, S4’s speed and memory use is competitive with the most efficient Transformer variants benchmarked by Tay et al. (2021)— Linear Transformer (Katharopoulos et al., 2020) and Performer (Choromanski et al., 2020)—in a parameter-matched setting (Table 2, following the protocol of Tay et al. (2021)).

## 4.2 LEARNING LONG RANGE DEPENDENCIES

As described in Sections 2.2 and 3.1, S4 uses a principled approach to address LRDs based on the HiPPO theory of continuous-time memorization. Our goal in this section is to validate that S4 achieves high performance on difficult tasks that require long-range reasoning. We focus here on two problems: (i) the Long-Range Arena, a well-known benchmark designed to test efficient sequence models on LRDs, and (ii) a speech classification problem as a real-world test of LRDs.

**Long Range Arena (LRA).** LRA (Tay et al., 2021) contains 6 tasks with lengths 1K-16K steps, encompassing modalities and objectives that require similarity, structural, and visuospatial reasoning. Table 3 compares S4 against the 11 Transformer variants from Tay et al. (2021) as well as follow-up work. S4 substantially advances the SoTA, outperforming all baselines on all tasks and averaging 80 _._ 48% compared to less than 60% for every baseline. Notably, S4 solves the Path-X task, an extremely challenging task that involves reasoning about LRDs over sequences of length 128 _×_ 128 = 16384. All previous models have failed (i.e. random guessing) due to memory or computation bottlenecks, or simply being unable to learn such long dependencies.

We analyze S4’s performance on Path-X by visualizing its learned representations, in particular 1-D convolution kernels _**K**_ which are the focus of our technical results in Section 3. Fig. 2 shows that S4

7

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

learns a variety of filters that display spatially consistent structure and demonstrate awareness of the 2-D nature of the data. In particular, the lower layers learn simple kernels that extract features from just a few rows of local context while ignoring the rest of the image. On the other hand, higher layers aggregate information globally across full columns of the image at varying spatial frequencies. Filters in these higher layers span the entire context (16384 pixels), confirming S4’s ability to learn LRDs.

**Raw Speech Classification.** Speech is a typical real-world time series domain, involving signals sampled from an underlying physical process at high frequency. We perform speech classification using the _Speech Commands_ dataset (Warden, 2018). While most sequence models for speech rely on extensive preprocessing (e.g. to MFCC features), we classify raw speech (length-16000) following Romero et al. (2021). S4 achieves 98 _._ 3% accuracy, higher than all baselines that use the 100 _×_ shorter MFCC features, and validates that a powerful LRD model is able to extract more information from the raw data and outperform hand-crafted pre-processing. Additionally, we include a baseline CNN specifically designed for raw speech, the discriminator from the WaveGAN model (Donahue et al., 2019), which performs worse than S4 while having 90 _×_ more parameters and incorporating many more architectural heuristics (Appendix D.2).

## 4.3 S4 AS A GENERAL SEQUENCE MODEL

A key goal of sequence modeling research is to develop a single model that can be applied in many domains (e.g. images, audio, text, time-series) with a broad range of capabilities (e.g. efficient training, fast generation, handling irregularly sampled data). As a fundamental scientific model, SSMs are a promising candidate that come with a range of capabilities, and S4’s strong results on LRD benchmarks spanning images, text, and speech are evidence of S4’s potential as a general sequence model. In this section, we focus on understanding this question in more depth by highlighting key strengths of S4 in settings that usually require specialized models. The tasks we focus on (generative modeling, image classification, time-series forecasting) are considered as LRD tasks in the literature, and serve as additional validation that S4 handles LRDs efficiently.

**Large-scale generative modeling.** We investigate two well-studied image and text benchmarks to validate the scalability, flexibility, and efficiency of S4. These tasks require much larger models than our previous tasks – up to 250M parameters.

First, CIFAR density estimation is a popular benchmark for autoregressive models, where images are flattened into a sequence of 3072 RGB subpixels that are predicted one by one. Table 6 shows that _with no 2D inductive bias_ , S4 is competitive with the best models designed for this task.

Second, WikiText-103 is an established benchmark for language modeling, an important task for large-scale sequence models where tokens are predicted sequentially based on past context. Although RNNs were the model of choice for many years, Transformers are now the dominant model in such applications that contain data that is inherently discrete. We show that alternative models

to Transformers can still be competitive in these settings. By simply taking a strong Transformer baseline (Baevski & Auli, 2018) and replacing the self-attention layers, S4 substantially closes the gap to Transformers (within 0 _._ 8 ppl), setting SoTA for attention-free models by over 2 ppl.

**Fast autoregressive inference.** A prominent limitation of autoregressive models is inference speed (e.g. generation), since they require a pass over the full context for every new sample. Several methods have been specifically crafted to overcome this limitation such as the Linear Transformer, a hybrid Transformer/RNN that switches to a stateful, recurrent view at inference time for speed.

As a stateful model, SSMs automatically have this ability (Fig. 1). By switching to its recurrent representation (Section 2.3), S4 requires _constant memory and computation_ per time step – in contrast to standard autoregressive models which scale in the context length. On both CIFAR-10 and WikiText103, we report the throughput of various models at generation time, with S4 around 60 _×_ faster than a vanilla Transformer on both tasks (details in Appendix D.3.3).

**Sampling resolution change.** As a continuous-time model, S4 automatically adapts to data sampled at different rates, a challenging setting for time series with a dedicated line of work (Rubanova et al., 2019; De Brouwer et al., 2019; Romero et al., 2021). Without re-training, S4 achieves 96 _._ 3% accuracy at 0 _._ 5 _×_ the frequency on Speech Commands (Table 4), simply by changing its internal step size ∆ (Section 2.3).

**Learning with weaker inductive bias.** Beyond our results on speech (Section 4.2), we further validate that S4 can be applied with minimal modifications on two domains that typically require specialized domain-specific preprocessing and architectures. First, we compare S4 to the Informer (Zhou et al., 2021), a new Transformer architecture that uses a complex encoder-decoder designed for time-series forecasting problems. A simple application of S4 that treats forecasting as a masked sequence-to-sequence transformation (Fig. 3) outperforms the Informer and other baselines on 40 _/_ 50 settings across 5 forecasting tasks. Notably, S4 is better on the longest setting in each task, e.g. reducing MSE by 37% when forecasting 30 days of weather data (Appendix D.3.5).

Finally, we evaluate S4 on pixel-level sequential image classification tasks (Table 5), popular benchmarks which were originally LRD tests for RNNs (Arjovsky et al., 2016). Beyond LRDs, these benchmarks point to a recent effort of the ML community to solve vision problems with reduced domain knowledge, in the spirit of models such as Vision Transformers (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020) and MLP-Mixer (Tolstikhin et al., 2021) . Sequential CIFAR is a particularly challenging dataset where outside of SSMs, all sequence models have a gap of over 25% to a simple 2-D CNN. By contrast, S4 is competitive with a larger ResNet18 (7.9M vs. 11.0M parameters), both with ( **93.16%** vs. 95.62%) or without ( **91.12%** vs. 89.46%) data augmentation. Moreover, it is much more robust to other architectural choices (e.g. **90.46%** vs. 79.52% when swapping BatchNorm for LayerNorm).

## 5 CONCLUSION

We introduce S4, a sequence model that uses a new parameterization for the state space model’s continuous-time, recurrent, and convolutional views to efficiently model LRDs in a principled manner. Results across established benchmarks evaluating a diverse range of data modalities and model capabilities suggest that S4 has the potential to be an effective general sequence modeling solution.

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces S4, a structured state space model designed to efficiently handle long-range dependencies. The technical contribution is the Normal Plus Low-Rank (NPLR) parameterization of the HiPPO matrix, which allows for efficient computation via Cauchy kernels. The empirical results are strong, particularly on the Long Range Arena (LRA) benchmark. However, the paper overreaches in framing S4 as a 'general sequence model' capable of replacing specialized architectures, and it contains gaps regarding the numerical stability of the decomposition and the stability of the recurrent dynamics.

S4 represents a significant technical advancement in sequence modeling, offering a theoretically grounded solution to the computational bottleneck of prior SSM work. The paper clearly articulates the problem and provides a novel parameterization to solve it. However, the paper overstates its contribution by claiming S4 is a 'general-purpose' model, as the experiments focus almost exclusively on long-range tasks and fail to validate key capabilities mentioned in the introduction. Additionally, the narrative regarding 'weak inductive biases' contradicts the empirical results.

Overview issues:

- Overstated Scope of 'General' Capabilities: The paper claims S4 is a general-purpose sequence model capable of handling images, text, and time series with minimal modification (Section 1, Conclusion). However, the experiments primarily validate its performance on *long-range dependencies* (LRDs). The paper does not test S4 on tasks where LRDs are not the primary challenge, nor does it demonstrate the 'irregularly sampled data' capability mentioned in the introduction. This limits the contribution to efficient LRD modeling rather than a fully general solution.

- Unanalyzed Numerical Stability of NPLR Factors: The paper relies on the NPLR decomposition (Theorem 1) to avoid the ill-conditioning of diagonalizing the HiPPO matrix. However, the paper does not analyze the conditioning of the resulting diagonal matrix $ m{ m{	ext{Lambda}}}$ and low-rank factors $ m{P},  m{Q}$. If these factors are ill-conditioned, the subsequent Cauchy kernel computation (which relies on these factors) may still be numerically unstable. The paper assumes the stability of the decomposition is guaranteed by the form, but it does not prove or empirically verify that the factors themselves are well-conditioned for all $N$.

- Contradiction in Inductive Bias Narrative: The paper argues that S4 succeeds on sequential CIFAR and speech classification because it has 'weaker inductive biases' compared to specialized CNNs (Section 4.3). However, the results (S4 achieves 91.13% on Sequential CIFAR vs. 63.74% for LSSL) are *better* than the specialized CNNs (CKConv 99.32%, WaveGAN-D 96.25%) mentioned in the text. If S4 performs better than specialized models with strong biases, then S4 must actually be learning strong inductive biases that the authors attribute to the 'general' nature of the model. This contradicts the narrative that S4 succeeds *despite* lacking strong biases.

- Missing Stability Analysis of Recurrent Dynamics: The paper introduces S4 for both recurrent and convolutional computation. For the recurrent mode to be viable, the discretized matrix $ m{ m{	ext{overline{A}}}}$ (Equation 3) must have a spectral radius less than 1 to ensure state stability. The paper assumes stability based on the HiPPO theory but does not provide a proof or numerical verification of the spectral radius for the specific discretization scheme used (bilinear method). Without this analysis, the claim that S4 can be used as a stable RNN is unsupported.

Recommendation: Major Revision. The technical contribution is sound and the empirical results are strong, but the framing of S4 as a 'general sequence model' is misleading and contradicted by the experimental focus and narrative. The paper needs to narrow its claims to 'efficient long-range modeling' and address the stability and conditioning concerns.

Revision targets:

- Narrow the contribution claim from 'general sequence model' to 'efficient long-range dependency model' to align with the experimental scope.

- Add an analysis of the spectral radius of the discretized $ m{ m{	ext{overline{A}}}}$ matrix to support the claim of stability in the recurrent mode.

- Include an experiment or analysis demonstrating the 'irregularly sampled data' capability mentioned in the introduction to substantiate the 'general' claim.

comments.title: Scope of Generalization Claims
comments.quote: A central goal of sequence modeling is designing a single principled model that can address sequence data across a range of modalities and tasks, particularly on long-range dependencies.
comments.explanation: The abstract asserts that S4 is a 'single principled model that can address sequence data across a range of modalities and tasks'. However, the experiments primarily validate its performance on long-range dependencies (LRDs). The paper does not test S4 on tasks where LRDs are not the primary challenge, nor does it demonstrate the 'irregularly sampled data' capability mentioned in the introduction. This limits the contribution to efficient LRD modeling rather than a fully general solution.

comments.title: Numerical Stability of NPLR Factors
comments.quote: reducing the SSM to the well-studied computation of a Cauchy kernel
comments.explanation: The paper relies on the NPLR decomposition (Theorem 1) to avoid the ill-conditioning of diagonalizing the HiPPO matrix. However, the paper does not analyze the conditioning of the resulting diagonal matrix $\Lambda$ and low-rank factors $P, Q$. If these factors are ill-conditioned, the subsequent Cauchy kernel computation (which relies on these factors) may still be numerically unstable. The paper assumes the stability of the decomposition is guaranteed by the form, but it does not prove or empirically verify that the factors themselves are well-conditioned for all $N$.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces S4, a computationally efficient parameterization for state space models that achieves strong results on long-range dependency benchmarks. The theoretical derivation of the NPLR structure and its reduction to Cauchy kernels is mathematically sound and addresses a genuine bottleneck in prior work. The empirical evaluation across diverse modalities demonstrates the model's versatility and competitive performance. However, the manuscript would benefit from deeper analysis of training dynamics, fairer efficiency baselines, and a clearer discussion of practical scalability.

Overview issues:

- Lack of Analysis on Training Dynamics and Gradient Flow: The paper focuses almost exclusively on forward-pass efficiency and empirical accuracy. It completely omits any discussion of training stability or gradient flow. SSMs are continuous-time models discretized via the bilinear method (Eq. 3), which can introduce stiff dynamics or ill-conditioned Jacobians during backpropagation. Without analyzing how gradients propagate through the NPLR parameterization (Sec. 3.2), the claim that S4 is a viable general sequence model (Sec. 1) remains incomplete. Readers will naturally wonder whether the model suffers from vanishing gradients or requires specialized optimizers. The authors should include a section analyzing gradient norms, backpropagation complexity, and optimization stability across different tasks, ideally with visualizations or convergence curves.

- Misleading Efficiency Comparison with LSSL: Table 1 compares S4’s layer-level training speed against the LSSL, but the LSSL numbers appear to come from a naive O(N^2 L) implementation rather than its theoretically efficient variants. The authors dismiss prior LSSL algorithms as numerically unstable (Sec. 1) without providing empirical verification or a fair comparison to the best feasible LSSL implementation. This creates a strawman comparison that artificially inflates S4’s speed and memory advantages. Since the LSSL paper (Gu et al., 2021) did propose alternative computation paths, the efficiency claims need to be benchmarked against those methods or explicitly state that only the naive baseline is used. Correcting this comparison would ground the efficiency narrative in a more realistic baseline.

- Insufficient Treatment of Step Size Delta and Discretization: The discretization step size Delta plays a central role in translating the continuous-time SSM into a discrete sequence model (Eq. 3), yet the paper treats it as a fixed or loosely tuned hyperparameter. The authors claim S4 adapts to different sampling frequencies by simply changing Delta at test time (Sec. 4.3), but they do not analyze how Delta interacts with sequence length, model depth, or data modality. In language modeling or image classification, the notion of a time step is arbitrary, and performance can be highly sensitive to this choice. A rigorous ablation or theoretical discussion on how Delta should scale with L or N is missing. The revision should include a systematic study of Delta sensitivity and propose a principled initialization or parameterization strategy for it.

- Missing Ablation on the Low-Rank Correction: The core technical contribution is the Normal Plus Low-Rank (NPLR) parameterization, which decomposes the HiPPO matrix into a diagonal component and a low-rank correction (Theorem 1, Eq. 6). However, the paper provides no ablation study to isolate the contribution of the low-rank term versus the diagonal part. Subsequent work has shown that diagonal-only variants often match or exceed the full S4 model, suggesting the low-rank correction may add computational overhead without proportional gains. Without ablations comparing NPLR-S4 against diagonal-S4 or other simplified parameterizations, readers cannot assess whether the added complexity is justified. The authors should include experiments isolating each component of the parameterization to clarify its actual impact on performance and efficiency.

- Gap Between Asymptotic Complexity and Practical Scalability: The paper claims O(N+L) computation and memory usage (Theorem 3), but asymptotic bounds often hide large constants or hardware-specific bottlenecks that limit real-world deployment. S4 relies on fast Cauchy kernel multiplication and FFTs, which can suffer from poor cache locality, memory bandwidth constraints, or lack of optimized kernels on modern accelerators. The efficiency benchmarks in Table 1 and Table 2 measure single-layer or small-batch operations, but do not reflect end-to-end training times for the 250M parameter models used on CIFAR-10 and WikiText-103. Distributed training introduces communication overhead that is not addressed. The revision should include wall-clock training times for the large-scale experiments, discuss kernel implementation details, and clarify how the theoretical complexity translates to actual throughput on standard hardware.

- Missing concrete worked example of the Cauchy kernel reduction: The paper derives a complex reduction from the HiPPO state matrix to a Cauchy kernel via NPLR decomposition, Woodbury identity, and FFT, but never computes a fully specified small-scale example. Readers cannot easily verify how the parameters Λ, P, Q, B, C map to the final convolution kernel K̄ for a concrete sequence. Adding a worked example with N=2 or 3 and a short sequence length would let readers trace the algebra and understand how the low-rank correction interacts with the diagonal terms in practice. This is standard for theory-heavy sequence modeling papers and would significantly aid reproducibility and intuition.

- Missing analysis of NPLR rank scaling and failure boundaries: The efficiency claims rely heavily on the low-rank rank r being small, specifically r=1 or 2 for HiPPO matrices as stated in Theorem 1. The paper does not discuss what happens if r grows with N, which would degrade the Õ(N+L) complexity and weaken the core efficiency argument. A demonstration showing how performance and computation time scale as r increases, or a discussion of scenarios where the NPLR assumption breaks down, is missing. Without this, the claim that S4 is essentially tight for sequence models lacks a clear boundary of applicability.

- Missing discussion on numerical stability of the Woodbury inversion: The method relies on inverting matrices of the form (2/Δ)(1-ω)/(1+ω) - Λ within the Cauchy kernel computation. The paper asserts numerical stability but provides no analysis of the condition numbers of these matrices or how close the eigenvalues Λ can get to the evaluation points ω. In practice, near-singular inversions can cause training instability or require epsilon regularization, which directly affects the model's behavior. The authors should include a stability analysis or ablation on regularization strength for the Cauchy kernel inversion, as this impacts the reliability of the claimed implementation.

- Underdeveloped link between continuous memorization and discrete tasks: The paper grounds S4 in HiPPO theory for continuous-time memorization of polynomial projections, yet applies it to discrete tasks like language modeling and image classification without explaining how the continuous-time assumptions translate. The paper claims S4 is a general-purpose model but does not develop the implications of using a continuous-time framework on discrete, non-temporal data. A discussion on how the discretization step size Δ and the continuous-time basis functions interact with discrete token or pixel sequences would strengthen the claim that S4 is a principled general sequence model rather than an empirically tuned architecture.

Recommendation: Major revision. The theoretical contributions are solid, but the paper lacks critical analyses of training stability, step-size sensitivity, and component ablations that are necessary to validate the claims of a general-purpose sequence model. Addressing these gaps will strengthen the empirical foundation and clarify the practical impact of the proposed parameterization.

Revision targets:

- Add a dedicated section analyzing gradient flow, backpropagation complexity, and optimization stability for the S4 parameterization.

- Replace the LSSL efficiency comparison with a benchmark against the best feasible LSSL implementation or explicitly justify the naive baseline.

- Conduct a systematic ablation study isolating the diagonal and low-rank components of the NPLR parameterization to justify its added complexity.

- Include a rigorous sensitivity analysis of the discretization step size Delta across different sequence lengths and modalities, with recommendations for its initialization or parameterization.

- Provide wall-clock end-to-end training times and hardware utilization metrics for the large-scale experiments to bridge the gap between asymptotic complexity and practical deployment.

comments.title: Structural constraints on parameters
comments.quote: del, where $\bm{A},\bm{B},\bm{C},\bm{D}$ are parameters learned by gradient descent.
comments.explanation: This statement in Section 2.1 directly contradicts the paper's core contribution and title. If these matrices are fully dense and unconstrained, the model reduces to a standard continuous-time RNN with $O(N^2)$ complexity, completely weakening the efficiency claims. The technical sections clarify that $\\bm{A}$ is fixed to the HiPPO structure and $\\bm{B}, \\bm{C}$ are low-dimensional, but Section 2.1 omits this crucial distinction. Please revise this sentence to explicitly state that the matrices are constrained to structured forms to enable near-linear computation.

comments.title: Unverified diagonalization in Lemma 3.2
comments.quote: Lemma 3.2. The HiPPO matrix  $\mathbf{A}$  in equation (2) is diagonalized by the matrix  $V_{ij} = \binom{i+j}{i-j}$ .
comments.explanation: The assertion that the HiPPO matrix is diagonalized by this specific binomial coefficient matrix conflicts with established spectral results for the HiPPO-LegS construction. Standard literature characterizes the eigenvectors via Legendre polynomials or hypergeometric functions. While the asymptotic bound on the entries is correct, the explicit formula lacks a derivation or citation. Please provide a reference or a brief derivation to support this claim, or replace it with the standard Legendre-based characterization.

comments.title: Contradiction in sequential CIFAR gap claim
comments.quote: Sequential CIFAR is a particularly challenging dataset where outside of SSMs, all sequence models have a gap of over  $25\%$  to a simple 2-D CNN. By contrast,
comments.explanation: Table 5 reports the best non-SSM baseline (LSSL) at 84.65% and S4 at 91.13%. A standard 2D CNN on CIFAR-10 typically reaches 90–95% accuracy. The actual gap between prior sequence models and a 2D CNN is roughly 5–10%, not 25%. This overstatement misrepresents the performance of prior work and artificially inflates S4's relative advantage. Please adjust the text to accurately reflect the empirical results presented in Table 5.

comments.title: 'Skip connection' mischaracterization of D
comments.quote:
omit the parameter $\bm{D}$ for exposition (or equivalently, assume $\bm{D}=0$) because the term $\bm{D}u$ can be viewed as a skip connection and is easy to compute.

###
comments.explanation: Labeling $\\bm{D}u$ as a skip connection conflates control-theoretic direct feedthrough with deep learning residual connections. In state-space theory, $\\bm{D}$ governs instantaneous input-output coupling; setting it to zero enforces a relative degree of at least one. This is a non-trivial modeling assumption that impacts short-term dependency capture. Please replace the 'skip connection' analogy with a precise justification for omitting the direct feedthrough term.

comments.title: Exaggerated efficiency claim
comments.quote: Table 1 confirms that the S4 is orders of magnitude more speed- and memory-efficient for practical layer sizes.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'orders of magnitude' typically implies a factor of 10 or more. Table 1 shows speed ratios of 1.9× at dim 128 and 6.7× at dim 256, which fall short of this threshold. Only at dim 512 does the speed ratio reach 29.6×. The memory ratios are consistently above 40×, so the memory claim holds, but the speed claim is overstated for smaller dimensions. Please revise the sentence to accurately reflect the data.

comments.title: AVG column calculation mismatch
comments.explanation: The AVG column does not match the arithmetic mean of the five reported task accuracies. For the Transformer, the listed values sum to 271.94, yielding a 5-task average of 54.39, not 53.66. The same discrepancy appears for other models like Reformer and BigBird. This suggests either an unreported weighting scheme or a calculation error. Please clarify how the AVG column is computed or provide the missing values to ensure reproducibility.

comments.title: Non-monotonic training step times
comments.explanation: The reported training step times for S4 decrease from dim 128 to 256 before rising again. While benchmark variance or GPU occupancy effects can cause this, it contradicts the expected monotonic scaling with model dimension and may confuse readers evaluating the $O(N+L)$ complexity claims. Add a brief note in the table caption or text explaining the experimental setup that accounts for this non-monotonic behavior.

comments.title: Ambiguous 1D/2D kernel mapping
comments.quote: learns a variety of filters that display spatially consistent structure and demonstrate awareness of the 2-D nature of the data.
comments.explanation: The passage claims that 1-D convolution kernels demonstrate 'awareness of the 2-D nature of the data' by extracting local row features and aggregating globally across columns. However, the Path-X benchmark flattens 128×128 images into a 1-D sequence. A 1-D kernel operates strictly along this flattened sequence. Without specifying the flattening order, the claimed spatial behavior is mathematically inconsistent. Please clarify the exact sequence ordering used and explicitly map the 1-D kernel weights to the 2-D spatial operations described.

comments.title: HiPPO as an SSM representation
comments.quote: addressing LRD s with the HiPPO framework
comments.explanation: The phrasing incorrectly elevates HiPPO to the status of an SSM representation, placing it on equal footing with continuous-time and discrete-time formulations. HiPPO is fundamentally a framework for optimal polynomial projection of continuous signals, not a distinct dynamical representation. Additionally, describing it as primarily 'addressing LRD' misstates its original scope, which focused on continuous signal compression. Please clarify that HiPPO serves as a principled parameterization or initialization strategy for the state matrix $\\mathbf{A}$, rather than listing it as a parallel representation type. Also, fix the stray 's' in 'LRD s'.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces the Structured State Space (S4) model, a novel parameterization of state-space models that enables efficient computation through a combination of normal-plus-low-rank (NPLR) decomposition and Cauchy kernel evaluation. By addressing the numerical instability of prior SSM approaches, the authors achieve state-of-the-art results on long-range dependency benchmarks like LRA.

The paper provides a significant technical contribution by solving the computational and numerical bottlenecks in applying deep state-space models to long sequences. The methodology is well-grounded in linear algebra, and the empirical results on challenging datasets like Path-X are impressive. It successfully bridges the gap between continuous-time modeling and efficient deep learning architectures.

Overview issues:

- Numerical Stability of Cauchy Kernel Evaluation: Algorithm 1 relies on computing the SSM generating function via Cauchy kernel evaluation. While the paper cites Pan (2001, 2017) for stability, it does not explicitly address the potential for catastrophic cancellation when evaluating the generating function at roots of unity, especially as the sequence length L grows to 16k or beyond. Given the non-normal nature of the underlying HiPPO matrices, the authors should provide a more detailed analysis or empirical evidence that the precision of the inverse FFT remains sufficient for deep stacks. A simple plot showing the condition number of the Cauchy matrix across different sequence lengths would clarify the limits of this approach.

- Generalization of NPLR Parameterization: Theorem 1 establishes that HiPPO matrices have an NPLR representation, which is central to the efficiency of S4. However, the paper does not discuss whether this NPLR structure is preserved or even meaningful when the model is trained on tasks that do not inherently align with the HiPPO basis (e.g., standard language modeling). If the learned A matrix deviates significantly from the initial NPLR structure during gradient descent, the efficiency guarantees in Theorems 2 and 3 may be compromised. The authors should clarify if the NPLR constraint is strictly enforced throughout training and what happens to performance if the low-rank correction term becomes large.

- Lack of Ablation on NPLR Parameterization: The NPLR parameterization is the core technical innovation, yet the paper lacks an ablation study comparing it to alternative structured matrices (e.g., purely diagonal or circulant matrices). It is unclear how much of the performance gain on LRA tasks is attributable to the specific HiPPO initialization versus the flexibility of the NPLR structure itself. Adding a baseline that uses a simpler diagonal-only SSM with the same HiPPO initialization would help isolate the benefit of the low-rank correction term.

- Hardware-Specific Efficiency Discrepancies: The paper claims S4 is hardware-efficient, but Table 2 shows that for short sequences (1024), S4 is not significantly faster than efficient Transformers. The efficiency gains are mostly realized at longer sequence lengths where FFT-based convolution dominates. A more transparent discussion of the overhead associated with the complex-valued arithmetic required by the S4 parameterization on standard GPU kernels would be beneficial. The authors should explicitly state if custom CUDA kernels were required to achieve the reported speedups, as this impacts the reproducibility of the efficiency claims.

- Lack of empirical demonstration for sampling resolution change: The paper claims S4 can adapt to different sampling frequencies without retraining as a key advantage of its continuous-time formulation. However, this capability is only demonstrated on a single speech classification task. To substantiate the claim that this is a general-purpose feature, the authors should provide a controlled experiment showing the degradation of performance as a function of sampling rate deviation (e.g., 0.25x to 2.0x) on a secondary task, such as time-series forecasting. This would confirm that the discretization step size $\Delta$ effectively maps the learned continuous dynamics to new temporal resolutions without requiring fine-tuning.

- Missing analysis of the learnable step size $\Delta$: The step size $\Delta$ is a critical parameter in the discretization of the continuous SSM, yet the paper does not analyze how its value evolves or is initialized across different tasks. It is unclear if $\Delta$ is learned as a global scalar, per-layer, or per-feature, and whether this choice significantly impacts the model's ability to capture long-range dependencies. A brief empirical study or plot showing the distribution of $\Delta$ values across layers after training on a task like Path-X would provide necessary insight into how S4 adapts its temporal resolution to the input data.

- Absence of a concrete example for the NPLR decomposition: While Theorem 1 provides the theoretical basis for the NPLR decomposition, the paper lacks a worked example showing how a specific non-HiPPO matrix might be represented in this form. Providing a concrete, small-scale example (e.g., for $N=4$) of a matrix that is not initially in HiPPO form but can be decomposed into a normal plus low-rank structure would help readers understand the flexibility of the proposed parameterization. This would clarify whether the NPLR structure is a restrictive assumption or a broad class of matrices that S4 can effectively optimize.

Recommendation: accept

Revision targets:

- Include an empirical analysis of the numerical condition number of the Cauchy matrix as sequence length increases.

- Clarify whether the NPLR structure is maintained as a hard constraint during training or if the matrix A is allowed to deviate.

- Add a brief discussion or supplementary figure regarding the hardware-specific overhead of complex-valued S4 operations compared to standard real-valued Transformer operations.

comments.title: Inaccurate description of S4 parameterization
comments.quote: k. Technically, S4 reparameterizes the structured state matrices  $\mathbf{A}$  appearing in Voelker et al. (2019); Gu et al. (2020a) by decomposing them as the sum of a low-rank and skew-symmetric term. A
comments.explanation: The paper describes the S4 parameterization as a decomposition into a low-rank and skew-symmetric term. However, Section 3.2 defines the S4 parameterization as a Normal Plus Low-Rank (NPLR) structure, where the normal part is diagonal. A skew-symmetric matrix is a specific type of normal matrix, but the general S4 parameterization uses a diagonal matrix plus a low-rank term. Describing it as 'skew-symmetric' is misleading and technically narrower than the actual implementation.

comments.title: Ambiguity in computational complexity claims
comments.quote: gths. Our technique involves conditioning $A$ with a low-rank correction, allowing it to be diagonalized stably and reducing the SSM to the well-studied computation of a Cauchy kernel.
comments.explanation: The claim that the low-rank correction allows the matrix A to be 'diagonalized stably' is misleading. While the NPLR parameterization allows for efficient computation via the Cauchy kernel, the stability of the diagonalization itself depends on the condition number of the transformation matrix V. As noted in Lemma 3.2, the HiPPO matrix V has entries of magnitude up to 2^(4N/3), implying the transformation is inherently ill-conditioned for large N. Please clarify that the stability is a property of the Cauchy kernel computation rather than the diagonalization of A itself.

comments.title: Unjustified assumption of Woodbury identity applicability
comments.quote: low-rank term can now be corrected by applying the Woodbury identity which reduces  $(A + PQ^{*})^{-1}$  in terms of  $A^{-1}$ , truly reducing to the diagonal case.
comments.explanation: The Woodbury matrix identity requires the matrix A (the normal part) to be invertible. If A is singular, the identity cannot be applied directly. Given that HiPPO matrices may have specific spectral properties, please explicitly state the condition that A is assumed to be invertible or clarify that the identity is applied to a shifted matrix (A - lambda I) where lambda is chosen to ensure invertibility.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the definition of the Cauchy kernel
comments.quote: }(\omega) & k_{01}(\omega) \\ k_{10}(\omega) & k_{11}(\omega) \end{bmatrix} \gets \left[\widetilde{C} Q\right]^* \left(\frac{2}{\Delta} \frac{1 - \omega}{1 + \omega} - \Lambda\right)^{-1} [B P]
comments.explanation: The notation [C Q]* and [B P] implies block concatenation, but the dimensions of the resulting matrices are not explicitly constrained. Specifically, C and B are N-dimensional vectors, while P and Q are N x R matrices. For the matrix product to be well-defined as a 2x2 block matrix, the dimensions of the concatenated blocks must be compatible with the N x N diagonal matrix Lambda. Please clarify the dimensions of the concatenated blocks and the resulting matrix product to ensure the 2x2 output is mathematically sound.

comments.title: Ambiguity in parameter count units
comments.quote: These comprise S4’s $5N$ trainable parameters.
comments.explanation: The parameter count of 5N is derived from complex-valued components. In deep learning, parameter counts are typically reported in terms of real-valued scalars. Since each complex parameter consists of two real degrees of freedom, the actual count of real-valued trainable parameters is 10N. Please clarify that 5N refers to the count of complex parameters to avoid misinterpretation.

comments.title: Clarification of indexing in the convolution kernel definition
comments.quote: \overline{\bm{K}}\in\mathbb{R}^{L}:=\mathcal{K}_{L}(\overline{\bm{A}},\overline{\bm{B}},\overline{\bm{C}}):=\left(\overline{\bm{C}\bm{A}}^{i}\overline{\bm{B}}\right)_{i\in[L]}
comments.explanation: The index set [L] is often defined as {1, ..., L}, but the expansion provided shows the sequence starting with the power i=0. To avoid ambiguity, replace the index set notation [L] with {0, ..., L-1} to explicitly match the powers of A used in the kernel expansion.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces S4, a structured state space model that achieves efficient computation via a Normal Plus Low-Rank (NPLR) parameterization. The core claim is that S4 can be computed in O(L log L) time via a Cauchy kernel, enabling strong performance on long-range dependency benchmarks. While the empirical results are impressive, several critical issues arise regarding the rigor of the theoretical derivations, the practical stability of the algorithms, and the fairness of the experimental comparisons.

The paper makes a significant contribution by proposing a computationally efficient parameterization of state space models that achieves strong empirical results on long-range dependency tasks. The key ideas—NPLR decomposition, generating function computation, and reduction to Cauchy kernels—are clever and well-motivated. However, the theoretical claims lack full rigor, the numerical stability of the proposed algorithms is not adequately addressed, and some experimental comparisons may overstate the advantages of S4 due to uncontrolled factors.

Overview issues:

- Incomplete derivation of O(L log L) convolution complexity: Theorem 3 claims that the SSM convolution filter can be computed in O(N+L) operations, but the derivation relies on the Fast Multipole Method (FMM) for Cauchy matrices. The paper references Pan (2001, 2015, 2017) without providing a self-contained argument or specifying which variant of the FMM applies. The complexity analysis in Appendix C is referenced but not included in the main text, making it impossible for readers to verify the claim. Moreover, the O(N+L) bound appears to ignore logarithmic factors that are inherent in FMM-based approaches. The paper should either provide a clear sketch of the algorithm's complexity or explicitly state the polylogarithmic factors.

- Numerical stability of the S4 convolution algorithm is unverified: Algorithm 1 involves computing a Cauchy kernel and applying the Woodbury identity, both of which can be numerically unstable for ill-conditioned matrices. The paper acknowledges that the HiPPO matrix is highly non-normal and that naive diagonalization leads to exponential entries (Lemma 3.2), but does not analyze the condition number of the NPLR decomposition or the Cauchy kernel. The Woodbury identity requires inverting a matrix that may be nearly singular, especially for large state sizes N. Without error analysis or empirical validation of numerical precision (e.g., forward/backward error), the practical reliability of the algorithm is unclear. The paper should include a numerical stability analysis or at least report condition numbers for typical parameter ranges.

- Equivalence of convolutional and recurrent representations during training is not guaranteed: The paper claims that S4 can switch between convolutional (training) and recurrent (inference) representations, implying they are exactly equivalent. However, the convolutional representation requires computing the full kernel K of length L, while the recurrent representation uses a truncated or approximate kernel in practice. If the kernel computation in Algorithm 1 involves approximations (e.g., truncation of the generating function or inexact FMM), the two representations may not be identical, potentially causing a train-test mismatch. The paper does not discuss this issue or provide empirical evidence that the representations are consistent. The authors should clarify whether the kernel computation is exact or approximate, and if approximate, quantify the error and its impact on downstream performance.

- Empirical comparisons do not control for model size and training budget: Tables 3, 4, and 5 compare S4 against a wide range of baselines, but the paper does not consistently report the number of parameters, training steps, or computational budget for each method. For example, in the Long Range Arena (Table 3), S4 uses a different architecture (state space model) than the Transformer variants, and it is unclear whether the comparison is parameter-matched or compute-matched. The paper claims S4 is 'as efficient as all competitors' (Table 2) but only reports relative speed and memory for a single configuration. Without controlling for these factors, the reported performance gains may be due to larger model capacity or longer training rather than architectural superiority. The authors should provide parameter counts and training budgets for all baselines, or perform controlled experiments where model size and compute are held constant.

- The HiPPO initialization may not transfer to discrete-time settings: The paper relies heavily on the HiPPO matrix (2) for initializing the state matrix A, which was derived for continuous-time memorization. The discretization via the bilinear transform (3) may alter the spectral properties of A, potentially degrading the long-range memory guarantees. The paper does not analyze how the discretization step size Δ affects the effective memory of the SSM, nor does it provide guidelines for choosing Δ. In the speech classification experiment (Table 4), S4 achieves 98.32% accuracy on raw audio, but the paper does not report the Δ values used or their sensitivity. The authors should include an ablation study on the effect of Δ and discuss whether the HiPPO initialization remains beneficial after discretization.

- Lack of ablation studies isolating the contribution of each component: The S4 model combines several innovations: the NPLR parameterization, the generating function approach, the Cauchy kernel reduction, and the HiPPO initialization. The paper does not ablate these components to determine which are essential for the reported performance. For instance, it is unclear whether the NPLR decomposition alone (without the generating function) would suffice, or whether the HiPPO initialization is necessary when using the NPLR parameterization. Without such ablations, readers cannot assess the incremental contribution of each technical idea. The authors should include experiments that remove or replace key components (e.g., using a random A matrix, or computing the kernel via naive multiplication) to demonstrate the necessity of each element.

- Generalization claims are overstated given limited task diversity: The paper positions S4 as a 'general sequence model' that can handle images, audio, text, and time-series with minimal modification. However, the empirical evaluation is limited to classification, density estimation, language modeling, and forecasting tasks that are all well-suited to the SSM's linear dynamics. The paper does not test on tasks that require complex relational reasoning (e.g., natural language inference, question answering) or compositional generalization (e.g., SCAN, COGS), where Transformers excel. Moreover, the time-series forecasting experiments (Section 4.3) are only briefly mentioned, with results deferred to the appendix. To support the claim of generality, the paper should include a broader set of tasks, particularly those that challenge the linear state space assumption.

- Missing numerical stability analysis for Algorithm 1: The paper claims that S4's convolution algorithm is 'theoretically stable' because it reduces to a Cauchy kernel, but it never verifies this claim numerically. Algorithm 1 involves computing a Cauchy kernel and applying the Woodbury identity, both of which can amplify errors when the HiPPO-derived matrices are ill-conditioned. The paper acknowledges that the HiPPO matrix is highly non-normal and that naive diagonalization leads to exponential entries (Lemma 3.2), but it does not report the condition number of the NPLR decomposition or the Cauchy kernel for typical state sizes N (e.g., N=64, 128, 256). Without a forward/backward error analysis or at least empirical measurements of numerical precision (e.g., comparing outputs in float32 vs. float64), readers cannot assess whether the algorithm is reliable in practice. The authors should compute and report the condition number of the Cauchy kernel for the HiPPO-initialized matrices, and show that the convolution kernel error stays below, say, 1e-6 for N up to 512.

- No ablation of the HiPPO initialization in S4: The paper attributes S4's long-range memory capability to the HiPPO initialization of the state matrix A, but it never tests whether this initialization is actually necessary when using the NPLR parameterization. The authors could train S4 with a random normal A matrix (or a random diagonal A) on a long-range task like Path-X or sequential CIFAR and compare performance. If the HiPPO initialization is essential, the paper should show that random initialization fails; if it is not, then the paper's emphasis on HiPPO as a key component is misleading. This ablation is standard for papers that propose a new parameterization and claim it preserves the benefits of a prior initialization scheme.

- No discussion of how to choose the step size Δ: The discretization step size Δ is a critical hyperparameter in S4: it controls the effective memory of the SSM and is changed at test time to handle sampling rate changes (Section 4.3). However, the paper never explains how Δ is set during training or how it should be chosen for a new task. In the speech classification experiment, S4 achieves 98.32% accuracy on raw audio, but the paper does not report the Δ values used or whether they were tuned. Without guidance on Δ selection, practitioners cannot apply S4 to new problems. The authors should either provide a default heuristic (e.g., Δ = 1 / input frequency) or show that performance is robust to Δ over a reasonable range.

- Missing controlled comparison on LRA benchmarks: Table 3 shows S4 outperforming Transformer variants by 20+ points on average on the Long Range Arena, but the paper does not report the number of parameters, training steps, or computational budget for each method. Without this information, it is impossible to tell whether S4's gains come from its architecture or from using a larger model or more training. The authors should provide a table with parameter counts and training FLOPs for all methods in Table 3, or run a controlled experiment where model size and training budget are matched. This is standard practice for top-tier ML conferences.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a self-contained derivation of the O(N+L) complexity for the S4 convolution, including the polylogarithmic factors and a clear description of the FMM variant used.

- Include a numerical stability analysis of Algorithm 1, reporting condition numbers or forward/backward errors for typical N and L values, and discuss potential failure modes.

- Add controlled experiments that match parameter counts and training budgets between S4 and baselines on at least the LRA benchmark, and report these details for all experiments.

- Conduct ablation studies that isolate the effect of the NPLR decomposition, the generating function approach, and the HiPPO initialization on a representative task (e.g., sequential CIFAR-10 or Path-X).

- Clarify whether the convolutional and recurrent representations are exactly equivalent or approximate, and if approximate, quantify the approximation error and its impact on performance.

comments.title: Unclear justification for DPLR representation of HiPPO matrix
comments.quote: . By Lemma 3.1 and Theorem 1, this SSM is unitarily equivalent to some $(\bm{\Lambda}-\bm{P}\bm{Q}^{*},\bm{B},\bm{C})$ for some diagonal $\bm{\Lambda}$ and vectors $\bm{P},\bm{Q},\bm{B},\bm{C}\in\mathbbm{C}^{N\times 1}$.
comments.explanation: The HiPPO matrix is not normal, so Theorem 1 (which applies to normal-plus-low-rank matrices) does not directly apply. The paper does not specify a similarity transform that converts the HiPPO matrix into a normal-plus-low-rank form, nor does it cite a specific lemma that would justify this step. Without this justification, the claim that the SSM is unitarily equivalent to the DPLR form is unsupported. Rewrite the passage to explicitly state the similarity transform used and cite the relevant result that guarantees such a representation for the HiPPO matrix.

comments.title: Parameter count ambiguity: complex vs. real parameters
comments.quote: These comprise S4’s $5N$ trainable parameters.
comments.explanation: The representation involves a diagonal $\bm{\Lambda}$ (N complex parameters) and vectors $\bm{P},\bm{Q},\bm{B},\bm{C}$ (each N complex parameters), totaling $5N$ complex parameters. Since the original SSM is real-valued, unitary equivalence may introduce complex numbers, so the number of real parameters would be $10N$ unless constraints are imposed. The text does not clarify whether the parameters are complex or real, nor does it explain why only $5N$ real parameters suffice. Add a note specifying that the parameters are complex and adjust the count to $10N$ real parameters, or explain why only $5N$ real parameters are needed (e.g., if $\bm{\Lambda}$ is real and $\bm{P},\bm{Q},\bm{B},\bm{C}$ are real).

comments.title: Robustness claim based on single comparison
comments.quote: over, it is much more robust to other architectural choices (e.g.  $90.46\%$  vs.  $79.52\%$  when swapping BatchNorm for LayerNorm).
comments.explanation: The claim that S4 is 'much more robust to other architectural choices' is based on a single comparison (BatchNorm vs. LayerNorm) on one dataset (sequential CIFAR). This is insufficient to support a general claim of robustness. The authors should either provide more extensive ablation studies (e.g., varying normalization, activation functions, depth) across multiple tasks, or qualify the claim (e.g., 'S4 appears robust to the choice of normalization on this task'). Rewrite the sentence to say: 'Moreover, S4 appears robust to the choice of normalization on this task (e.g., 90.46% vs. 79.52% when swapping BatchNorm for LayerNorm).'

comments.title: Missing justification for resolution change claim
comments.quote: Without re-training, S4 achieves  $96.3\%$  accuracy at  $0.5 \times$  the frequency on Speech Commands (Table 4), simply by changing its internal step size  $\Delta$  (Section 2.3).
comments.explanation: The claim that S4 adapts to different sampling rates by changing the step size $\Delta$ is plausible given the continuous-time formulation, but the paper does not explain how $\Delta$ is adjusted for the $0.5\times$ frequency case. Is $\Delta$ doubled? Is there a theoretical justification that the model's output remains consistent under such a change? The authors should provide the specific formula or procedure for adjusting $\Delta$ and ideally a brief justification (e.g., by rescaling the continuous-time dynamics). Add a sentence after the quoted text: 'Specifically, for a frequency change by factor $\alpha$, we set $\Delta \leftarrow \Delta/\alpha$ to maintain the same continuous-time dynamics.'

comments.title: Table 7 caption garbled by OCR
comments.quote: Table 7: (WikiText-103 language modeling) S4 approaches the performance of Transformers with much our implementation is based on, with attention replaced by S4. (Bottom) Attention-free models (RNNs and CNNs).
comments.explanation: The caption for Table 7 contains garbled text: 'with much our implementation is based on, with attention replaced by S4.' This is clearly an OCR artifact or editing error. The intended caption likely should read something like 'S4 approaches the performance of Transformers, whose implementation we base ours on, with attention replaced by S4.' The current text is ungrammatical and confusing. Rewrite the caption to be clear and grammatically correct, e.g., 'S4 approaches the performance of Transformers, with attention replaced by S4. (Bottom) Attention-free models (RNNs and CNNs).'
